html:
Roofing for General Manufacturing and Processing Plants in Spartanburg County

Spartanburg County's manufacturing base runs wider than the automotive supply chain that gets most of the attention. Textile finishing, chemical processing, plastics, and general fabrication plants operate throughout the county, many in buildings that have been added onto and re-equipped for decades. We roof that inventory with the same procurement discipline as a new-construction plant, adjusted for what each building's history actually left behind.

Reading a Plant Roof Before Quoting It

A manufacturing roof rarely matches its own drawings after twenty or thirty years of equipment changes. Rooftop curbs get added for new process exhaust, old penetrations get patched rather than properly flashed, and insulation gets buried under successive recover layers. We core-sample the existing assembly and map every curb and penetration before proposing a system, because a bid built on outdated drawings creates change orders once tear-off starts.

That mapping matters more on plants running heat-treating, machining coolant mist, or dust-generating processes, since airborne contaminants settle on the membrane and roof-mounted equipment differently than they do on a clean office building.

Older textile finishing plants in particular often carry a mix of deck types across a single building footprint, original construction on one section and a steel-frame addition bolted on decades later for a new process line. We survey each section on its own terms rather than assuming a single deck type carries across the whole roof, since a bid built on that assumption misses structural and drainage differences that show up the moment tear-off starts.

FM Global Ratings and Insurance-Driven Specs

Many manufacturing occupancies in this county carry FM Global coverage, and FM's approval standards for roof assemblies are often stricter than base code minimums, particularly around combustible insulation and Class 1 fire rating in specific occupancy classes. We check the plant's insurance carrier and FM classification before finalizing a spec, since a roof that passes code but fails the carrier's own approval list creates a coverage problem the facility does not discover until a claim.

Process Equipment and Roof Penetrations

Exhaust stacks, dust collection ductwork, and process piping cut through manufacturing roofs at a rate an office building never sees, and each penetration is a warranty exclusion point if it is not detailed correctly. We flash every penetration to the membrane manufacturer's published detail rather than a field improvisation, and we document penetration count and location in the closeout package so future roof work starts from an accurate map instead of a guess.

Recover Versus Full Replacement

Plants operating on tight capital budgets frequently ask whether a recover extends service life enough to defer full replacement. We answer that by pulling core samples to check for trapped moisture in the existing insulation, since a recover installed over a wet substrate fails faster than the membrane it replaced. Where the deck and insulation test dry and structurally sound, a recover can be the right economic call; where moisture is already present, we say so before quoting rather than after installation.

Scheduling Around Shift Production

Continuous-run plants rarely have a true down period, and even facilities that shut down for maintenance windows only give a roofing crew a narrow slot to work in. We build the project schedule around the plant's own maintenance calendar and confirm access, crane staging, and material delivery windows with the facilities manager before mobilizing.

Where a plant has no true shutdown window at all, we phase the work into zones sized to what can be torn off, dried in, and closed out inside a single scheduled maintenance pause, rather than proposing a timeline the plant cannot actually accommodate.

  • Core sampling and moisture survey of the existing roof assembly before bid
  • FM Approvals verification against the plant's insurance carrier requirements
  • Manufacturer-detail flashing at every process penetration and curb
  • Recover versus replacement analysis backed by substrate testing, not assumption
  • Scheduling coordinated to the plant's maintenance and production calendar
  • As-built penetration map delivered at project closeout

Manufacturing Roof Warranty Terms

Manufacturing occupancies put unusual stress on a membrane compared to standard commercial use, so we walk through warranty exclusions specific to that use, particularly around chemical exposure, exhaust residue, and rooftop equipment access, before a plant signs off on a system. An NDL warranty is only as good as the exclusions written into it, and we make sure the facilities director understands what is and is not covered before the contract is signed.

Manufacturing Plant Roofing Questions

Do you test for moisture before recommending a recover?

Yes, we pull core samples across the field before proposing a recover. A recover over a wet substrate is a warranty and performance problem we will not put our name on.

Can you work with our plant's FM Global requirements?

We check FM Approvals status on the assembly we specify and confirm it against the plant's own carrier requirements before finalizing the bid.

How do you handle exhaust and dust collection penetrations?

Every penetration gets flashed to the membrane manufacturer's published detail and logged in the as-built map, since undocumented penetrations are the most common warranty exclusion point.

What if our roof has been recovered multiple times already?

We core through all existing layers to confirm deck condition and total assembly weight before proposing another recover, since some roofs have reached the point where the deck can no longer support an additional layer.

Can you schedule around a maintenance shutdown window?

Yes, we build the project plan around the plant's own shutdown calendar rather than asking the plant to work around a standard construction schedule.
